notes.anchor: declared a public enemy by the Senate on 30 June by unanimous vote
notes.note: Renders hostis iudicatus: a formal Senate decree branding a citizen an enemy of the state, stripping his legal protections. Lepidus was so declared on 30 June 43 BC (Latin pridie Kalendas Quintilis) for joining Antony.
The Kalends were the first day of the Roman month; here 1 September 43 BC, the deadline by which Lepidus's followers could return to allegiance and escape the enemy declaration.

notes.anchor: by the letter you sent us from camp on the Nones of May
notes.note: The Nones fell on the 5th (or 7th) of the month; here 7 May 43 BC. Cassius's dispatch from his camp had convinced Rome that he had crushed Dolabella and was marching to Italy.

notes.anchor: had not Lepidus taken Antonius in --- stripped, unarmed, in flight
notes.note: After his defeat at Mutina Antony fled westward over the Alps; Lepidus received him and merged their armies in late May 43 BC, reviving a war Cicero thought already won.

notes.anchor: Against him we have the consuls-designate
notes.note: With both consuls of 43 (Hirtius and Pansa) dead at Mutina, Cicero looks to the consuls-designate for 42 BC --- D. Brutus and Plancus --- to oppose Lepidus in Gaul.
